subject: [PATCH v3 3/3] iova: defer maple tree erase on GFP_ATOMIC failure

2026-06-03.patch_summary: This patch adds a deferred free mechanism to handle GFP_ATOMIC failures during IOVA (I/O Virtual Address) allocation in the Linux kernel's iommu module. When GFP_ATOMIC fails, the IOVA entry remains in the maple tree and is added to a lockless per-domain deferred free list. A delayed workqueue retries the erase with GFP_ATOMIC after a 10ms delay, allowing for transient memory pressure to subside. This mechanism provides a safety net for exceptional cases where GFP_ATOMIC allocation fails, while minimizing corruption risks and ensuring double-free avoidance.

reviews.author: Jason Gunthorpe
reviews.summary: Reviewer Jason Gunthorpe questioned the use of mas_store(NULL) instead of mas_erase(), citing that iova alloc/free operations should be pairwise and not split allocations.
reviews.raw_body:
But why does it use mas_store(NULL) instead of mas_erase()? I thought
the iova alloc/free has to be pair wise, we don't split allocations?

Jason

2026-06-09.patch_summary: This patch adds a deferred free mechanism to handle GFP_ATOMIC failures during IOVA (I/O Virtual Address) allocation in the Linux kernel's iommu subsystem. When mas_store_gfp(NULL, GFP_ATOMIC) fails due to memory pressure, the IOVA entry remains in the maple tree and is added to a lockless per-domain deferred free list. A delayed workqueue retries the erase with GFP_ATOMIC after a 10ms delay, allowing the allocation to succeed when transient memory pressure subsides. This mechanism provides a safety net for rare GFP_ATOMIC erase failures, ensuring that IOVA address reuse and corruption are prevented.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledges Jason's suggestion to simplify Mas_erase() by moving the mas_wr_preallocate() call out of atomic context, agrees to try it in v4 and send a new patch if successful.
reviews.raw_body:
Mas_erase() would look cleaner, indeed, and it
looks like the mas_wr_preallocate() call is
safe from atomic context, as well!

Let me give that a try tomorrow.

I'll send a v4 if that cleanup works right.

-- 
All Rights Reversed.

2026-06-10.patch_summary: This patch adds a deferred free mechanism to handle GFP_ATOMIC failures during IOVA (I/O Virtual Address) allocation in the Linux kernel's iommu subsystem. When mas_store_gfp(NULL, GFP_ATOMIC) fails due to memory pressure, the IOVA entry remains in the maple tree and is added to a lockless per-domain deferred free list. A delayed workqueue retries the erase with GFP_ATOMIC after a 10ms delay, allowing the allocation to succeed when transient memory pressure subsides. This mechanism provides a safety net for rare GFP_ATOMIC erase failures, ensuring that IOVA address reuse and corruption are prevented.
